lesson.title: Writing your first program
masteryOutcomes: Write and execute a simple Python script using the print() function

concept: Using print() to display output in Python

explanations.text: A program is a list of instructions for the computer. The first one most people learn is print — it tells the computer to show some text on the screen.
print() is a built-in Python function. You write the word print, then parentheses, and inside the parentheses you put the value to display. Text values go in quotes, like print("Hello, world!").

print() takes one or more arguments, converts each to text, and writes them to standard output followed by a newline. The quotes mark a string literal; without them, Python treats the words as names to look up and raises an error.

hintLadder.text: You need the print function. What word comes before the parentheses?
Put the text you want to show inside the parentheses.
Text in Python must be wrapped in quotes — try "Hello, world!".

misconceptions.trigger: Writes print without parentheses, e.g. print "Hello, world!"
misconceptions.correction: In Python 3, print is a function, so it needs parentheses around its argument: print("Hello, world!").

misconceptions.trigger: Forgets the quotes, e.g. print(Hello, world!)
misconceptions.correction: Text must be a string in quotes. Without quotes Python looks for variables named Hello and world and fails — wrap it: print("Hello, world!").

checks.prompt: Write a line of Python that prints exactly: Hello, world!
acceptanceCriteria: Calls print as a function with parentheses
The argument is a string in quotes
checks.passFeedback: That runs and prints Hello, world! — your first working program.
checks.failFeedback: Not quite — check that you used print(...) with the text in quotes, then run it again.

practice.prompt: Write a program that prints your name.
acceptanceCriteria: The argument is a string in quotes
Runs without errors and prints a name

practice.prompt: In your own words, why does print("Hi") need the quotes?
rubric: Says the quotes mark text (a string)
Notes that without quotes Python treats the words as names and errors

practice.explanation: print is a function so it needs parentheses, and text must be in quotes; only print("Hello") has both.

pedagogyRules: Acknowledge the win before moving on.
When the student is wrong, point at the specific fix from the misconceptions, never just give the final answer.
In challenge mode, do not reveal the working line — guide with the hint ladder one step at a time.
Keep the student in the editor: ask them to run their own code rather than confirming it for them.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Read the real interpreter output. If it says missing parentheses or invalid syntax, name that exact cause; do not invent a different reason.
Only confirm success if the run output is exactly Hello, world!. If the output differs or errors, do not say it is correct.
Give one hint at a time from the ladder and let the student try between hints; never dump the full solution up front.

chunks.text: print() is a built-in Python function that displays text on the screen. You write print followed by parentheses, and put the value to show inside them.
Text values in Python are called strings and must be wrapped in quotes, for example "Hello, world!". Numbers do not need quotes.
In the Maestro Code Editor you type your code, press Run to execute it, and the result appears in the Output panel. Errors are shown there too.

celebration.headline: You wrote and ran your first program.
celebration.recap: You used print() to display text and saw it appear in the Output panel.
celebration.unlocked: Next: store and reuse values with variables.
